Double Letters (Digraphs)

Now that you know the 24 letters of the Greek alphabet, we need to talk about digraphs. Simply put, sometimes Greek takes two letters and glues them together to create a brand new sound.

This happens in English all the time. Think about the word s and h. Together, they form sh. The same principle applies in Greek, and there are two types: Vowel Combinations and Consonant Combinations.

1. Vowel Combinations (Diphthongs)

Greek loves vowel combinations, but here is the good news: thanks to a linguistic process over thousands of years, most of them just make an [ee] sound or an [e] sound.

Combination Pronunciation Example in English Greek Example Translation
αι [e] as in pet Egg αιτία (etía) cause
ει [ee] as in meet See είμαι (ímai) I am
οι [ee] as in meet See οικογένεια (ikoyénia) family
υι [ee] as in meet See υιοθετώ (io-thetó) to adopt
ου [oo] as in boot Moon ουρανός (ouranós) sky

[!TIP] Why all the "ee" sounds? You now know six different ways to write the [ee] sound in Greek (ι, η, υ, ει, οι, υι). - ει is mostly used for Verbs (e.g., έχει = he has). - οι is mostly used for Plural Nouns (e.g., φίλοι = friends). - ι / η are mostly used for Singular Nouns.

2. The Chameleons: αυ & ευ

The combinations αυ and ευ are special. The υ (Upsilon) completely loses its vowel sound and turns into a consonant—either a V or an F, depending on the letter that comes immediately after it.

The "F" Sound (Before Hard Consonants)

If the next letter is "hard" (voiceless), like κ, π, τ, θ, σ, φ, χ, the combination sounds like af or ef. - αυτοκίνητο ➔ (af-tokínito) = car - Ευρώπη ➔ (Ef-rópi) = Europe

The "V" Sound (Before Soft Consonants or Vowels)

If the next letter is a vowel or "soft" (voiced), like γ, β, δ, ζ, λ, μ, ν, ρ, it sounds like av or ev. - αύριο ➔ (áv-rio) = tomorrow - Ευαγγέλιο ➔ (Ev-angélio) = Gospel

3. Consonant Combinations

Ancient Greek didn't have the sounds for B, D, or G as we know them in English today. To make these sounds in Modern Greek, you have to smash two consonants together.

Combination Sound Greek Example Pronunciation Translation
μπ (m+p) B as in Boy μπαμπάς babás dad
ντ (n+t) D as in Dog ντύνω díno to dress
γκ / γγ (g+k) G as in Go άγκυρα ágira anchor
τσ (t+s) TS as in its τσέπη tsépi pocket
τζ (t+z) J / DZ as in jeep τζάκι dzáki fireplace

[!CAUTION] Wait, I thought Beta (Β) was 'B' and Delta (Δ) was 'D'? No! Remember from the previous lesson: Β sounds like V (Video), and Δ sounds like TH (There). So if you want to write "Bank" in Greek, you can't use Β. You have to use μπ ➔ Τράπεζα (Trápeza) or the English loan word Μπανκ.

Practice: Putting it all together!

Look at the word construction below and see how the digraphs shape the sound:

ντ (D) + ου (oo) + λ (l) + ά (a) + π (p) + α (a)
➔ ντουλάπα (dou-lá-pa) = closet

μπ (B) + ί (i) + ρ (r) + α (a)
➔ μπίρα (bí-ra) = beer
